coredata.dc:title: Sulphide/phosphide precipitation associated with carbon saturation in Ti–15V–3Cr–3Sn–3Al–0.2C
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, D.
Huang, A.J.
Song, X.P.
Wu, X.
coredata.dc:description:
Titanium sulphide precipitates, Ti3S, and sulphur-enriched titanium phosphide precipitates, Ti3(P, S), have been observed in a Ti–15V–3Cr–3Sn–3Al–0.2C alloy with sulphur less than 10wtppm and phosphorus of about 50wtppm. No such precipitates have been found in standard low C (90ppm) Ti-153. The titanium sulphide has a formula of Ti3S and an orthorhombic structure with lattice parameters of a =1.01nm, b =0.79nm and c =0.32nm determined using TEM diffraction analysis. The sulphur-enriched titanium phosphide has a formula of Ti3(P, S) with a P:S ratio of about 2:1. It has the same tetragonal structure as does the Ti3P and similar lattice parameters. Titanium sulphide precipitates have a well defined orientation relationship with the beta matrix which is
( 0
1
0 )
T
i 3
S
//{130}β and
[ 0
0
1 ]
T
i 3
S
//〈001〉β. Three variants of Ti3S precipitates, perpendicular to each other, were observed and their habit planes in the beta matrix are {130}. Ti3S has a morphology of a thin layer with a thickness along [010] axis of less than 50nm and up to 50 and 20μm in other two axes, respectively. The precipitation behaviour of the titanium sulphide during both continuous cooling and isothermal treatment is strongly affected by the carbon solution in the beta matrix.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Transition metal alloys and compounds
Precipitation
Crystal structure
TEM

The orientation relationship between Ti3S and the beta matrix in Ti–15V–3Cr–3Sn–3Al–0.2C. One of the specific forms is

(a) Many beam bright field TEM image showing a Ti3(P, S) precipitate in Ti–15V–3Cr–3Sn–3Al–0.2C after a heat treatment of 900°C/1h/WQ+400°C/2h/AC and (b–d) SAD patterns at B=[001], [101] and [111] zone axes.
SEM micrographs showing Ti–153–0.2C (a) air cooled and (b) furnace cooled from 1275°C. Furnace cooling was stopped at 700°C and the samples were air cooled to room temperature thereafter.
SEM micrographs showing the microstructures of Ti–153–0.2C plate furnace cooled from 1275°C to (a) 900°C and (b) 800°C then air cooled to room temperature.
SEM micrographs showing the microstructures of Ti–153–0.2C plate furnace cooled to 700°C after solution treatments of (a) 1150°C/1h and (b) 900°C/1h.

SEM images showing (a) long features associated with sulphide precipitates on the fracture surface of a Ti–153–0.2C sample aged at 400°C for 2h after 900°C for 1h. (b–d) Fracture surfaces of samples aged at 600°C for 2h after solution treatment at (b) 800°C, (c) 900°C and (d) 1150°C.

An enlarged area of Fig. 7a. The long features are grooves formed by those sulphide precipitates with their longest direction nearly perpendicular to the tension direction and their broad surfaces nearly parallel to the tension direction during bending.
Measured sulphide precipitates density (total length per unit area) against solution treatment temperature.
Observation of sulphide precipitates during furnace cooling with different starting and finishing temperatures
Observation of sulphide precipitates under different solution treatment (ST) and ageing (A) conditions

article.ce:title: Sulphide/phosphide precipitation associated with carbon saturation in Ti–15V–3Cr–3Sn–3Al–0.2C

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Interdisciplinary Research Centre in Materials, University of Birmingham, Edgbaston, Birmingham B15 2TT, UK

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: On leave from Beijing University of Science and Technology.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Titanium sulphide precipitates, Ti3S, and sulphur-enriched titanium phosphide precipitates, Ti3(P, S), have been observed in a Ti–15V–3Cr–3Sn–3Al–0.2C alloy with sulphur less than 10wtppm and phosphorus of about 50wtppm. No such precipitates have been found in standard low C (90ppm) Ti-153. The titanium sulphide has a formula of Ti3S and an orthorhombic structure with lattice parameters of a =1.01nm, b =0.79nm and c =0.32nm determined using TEM diffraction analysis. The sulphur-enriched titanium phosphide has a formula of Ti3(P, S) with a P:S ratio of about 2:1. It has the same tetragonal structure as does the Ti3P and similar lattice parameters. Titanium sulphide precipitates have a well defined orientation relationship with the beta matrix which is
( 0
1
0 )
T
i 3
S
//{130}β and
[ 0
0
1 ]
T
i 3
S
//〈001〉β. Three variants of Ti3S precipitates, perpendicular to each other, were observed and their habit planes in the beta matrix are {130}. Ti3S has a morphology of a thin layer with a thickness along [010] axis of less than 50nm and up to 50 and 20μm in other two axes, respectively.
The precipitation behaviour of the titanium sulphide during both continuous cooling and isothermal treatment is strongly affected by the carbon solution in the beta matrix.

ce:section.ce:para: Ti–15V–3Cr–3Sn–3Al (Ti-153) is one of the most successful beta titanium alloys developed in the 1980s [1,2]. It is a metastable beta strip alloy offering high strength after ageing and excellent cold deformability. The service condition of this alloy is solution treatment and ageing and a minimum of 8h ageing is required at the temperatures between 500 and 560°C to reach the maximum strength [3]. The slow ageing response is common and an intrinsic character in beta titanium alloys since the high concentration of beta stabilising alloying elements are added to retain the beta phase to room temperature. This has been tolerated in thick-sectioned forgings, since a good quenchability is of prime importance. However, in strip products in which quenchability is no longer the most important issue, acceleration in ageing response is needed to improve the efficiency of production. Recent work has demonstrated that the addition of a small amount of carbon into Ti-153 can promote recrystallisation and limit beta grain growth during processing, leading to widening of the processing windows for hot working. Carbon addition can also accelerate the ageing response significantly, reducing the ageing time for peak strength down to 1–2h, depending on ageing temperature [4,5]. However, precipitation of titanium sulphide and titanium phosphide was unexpectedly observed in carbon-doped Ti-153 alloys although the alloys have very low sulphur and phosphorus concentration. It is necessary to understand the reason behind sulphide/phosphide precipitation in alloys with low S and P concentration in order to advance the carbon-doping technology in beta alloys. This paper is aimed at identification of the precipitates and characterisation of their precipitation behaviours during continuous cooling and ageing, especially the influence of carbon addition.

Plates of standard Ti-153 (with 90ppm C) and of Ti–15V–3Cr–3Sn–3Al–0.2C (wt%) were supplied by Timet, USA as a research batch. They were hot rolled at 1075°C to 15mm. Chemical analysis shows that the alloy contains 0.203wt% carbon, 1020wtppm oxygen, 50wtppm phosphorus and less than 10wtppm sulphur. The carbide solvus in this alloy was determined as 1270°C in parallel work. Heat-treated samples were sectioned from the middle and ground and polished using standard procedures. Some samples were broken by bending using a vice to produce fracture surfaces. The polished surfaces and fracture surfaces were examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Thin foils for transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were prepared by twin-jet electrochemical thinning using a solution of 5% perchloric acid, 35% butan-1-o1 and 60% of methanol at a voltage of 30V at −30°C. TEM analysis was carried out in a FEI-Tecnai transmission electron microscope operating at 200kV or a CM20 microscope operating at 200kV. EDX analysis was carried out in the FEI-Tecnai microscope using Oxford LINK EDX system ISIS300 with a thin window detector.

Titanium sulphide precipitates were observed throughout the C-containing samples when they were heat treated appropriately but none was observed in similarly heat-treated standard (C <0.03wt%) Ti-153. One example is shown in Fig. 1 a where it is in its ‘edge-on’ orientation. The sample was water quenched from 900°C followed by ageing at 400°C for 2h. it can be seen that the precipitate is very thin, with a thickness of about 30nm. However, the other two dimensions can be up to 50μm in length and 20μm in width. The precipitates were found to have a formula of Ti3S using EDX in TEM. There are only limited crystal structural data in the literature about this compound. Murray in 1986 reported that Ti3S has a tetragonal structure based on an early X-ray work by Eremenko et al. [6,7], but no further information was given. However, in the present study this compound was found to have an orthorhombic crystal structure determined using TEM diffraction analysis. Fig. 1b shows some selected area diffraction (SAD) patterns from Ti3S precipitates and the indexing of the diffraction maxima. The lattice parameters was determined to be a =1.01nm, b =0.79nm and c =0.32nm by comparing with the beta matrix which has a lattice parameter of 0.323nm determined using X-ray diffraction in a parallel work [5]. The normal of the broad surface of the precipitates is parallel to the b-axis. There is an orientation relationship between the precipitates and the beta matrix which is shown in Fig. 2 and one specific form is
[ 0
0
1 ]
T
i 3
S
//[001]β and
( 0
1
0 )
T
i 3
S
//(130)β. The habit plane in the beta matrix is {130}. Three variants of Ti3S precipitates have been observed with them being perpendicular to each other.

The titanium phosphide precipitates are much rarer than the Ti3S precipitates. They are rod-like and about up to 0.5μm thick and a few microns long. One example is shown in Fig. 3a in which the length direction of the precipitate is close to the beam direction. Most of the phosphide precipitates were observed near beta grain boundaries. The titanium phosphide precipitates contain some sulphur and have a formula of Ti3(P, S) with a ratio of P:S of about 2:1. The Ti3(P,S) precipitates have been identified as having a tetragonal crystal structure with lattice parameters of a =0.99nm and c =0.50nm (again they were calculated using the beta matrix information), which is similar to the Ti3P phase. Fig. 3b–d shows the SAD patterns at three major zone axes.

Only the precipitation behaviour of titanium sulphide is studied in detail in this paper. Ti-153–0.2C samples were solution treated at 1275°C for 30min and subjected to air cooling (AC) and furnace cooling (FC). The cooling rate of furnace cooling was 10°Cmin−1. The carbide solvus of the this alloy was determined as 1270°C in parallel work [8]. Thus, carbon was in solution during solution treatment. Fig. 4 shows the air cooled and furnace cooled microstructures. In the air cooled samples (Fig. 4a), fine acicular precipitates are formed. Those acicular precipitates are titanium carbide. In the furnace cooled samples some long, straight and thin features were observed in addition to the titanium carbide precipitates, as shown in Fig. 4b. They are titanium sulphide precipitates. As revealed by TEM examination the thickness of the precipitates is less than 50nm and the contrast from the sulphide precipitates in Fig. 4b is clearly not simply an image of these thin precipitates but is likely to result from the fact that the samples have to be heavily etched to reveal the precipitates. The long and thin sulphide precipitates are distributed in the beta matrix fairly evenly.

The starting temperature of sulphide precipitation was estimated by furnace cooling samples to different temperatures and then cooling rapidly in air to room temperature. Fig. 5 shows the microstructures of samples furnace cooled to 900 and 800°C. There are no sulphide precipitates in the sample furnace cooled to 900°C as shown in Fig. 5a while there is a small number of them in the sample cooled to 800°C as shown in Fig. 5b. It can be seen that the density of the precipitates increases when the sample was furnace cooled to a low temperature by comparing Figs. 4b and 5b. The starting temperature of sulphide precipitation for furnace cooling is just above 800°C. The solution treatment temperature can affect precipitation of sulphide. Fig. 6a and b shows the microstructure of Ti-153-0.2C plate furnace cooled to 700°C after solution treatment at different temperatures. In the sample solution treated at 900°C the precipitates are not observed as shown in Fig. 6b while they are present in samples solution treated at 1150°C or higher, as shown in Fig. 6a. Thus, for furnace cooling (at a cooling rate of about 10°Cmin−1), there is a critical solution treatment temperature, above which precipitation will occur after solution treatment at temperature. The occurrence of sulphide precipitates during furnace cooling with different starting and finishing temperatures is summarised in Table 1 .

Sulphide precipitation during ageing in Ti-153–0.2C plates was investigated at temperatures of 400, 500, 600°C for 2h with different solution temperatures which were used to examine the effect of solution treatment. During ageing alpha phase precipitation also occurs, which makes observation of thin sulphide precipitates difficult on polished and etched samples. However, sulphide precipitates can be easily observed on fracture surfaces. Fig. 7a shows long features caused by the thin and long sulphide precipitates on the fracture surface of a sample subjected to bending to failure. The heat treatment of this sample was 900°C/1h/WQ+400°C/2h/AC. Those long features might be caused by debonding along the sulphide/matrix interfaces or cleavage of the precipitates themselves. In samples without sulphide precipitates, the fracture surfaces are full of normal dimples. One example is shown in Fig. 7b for which the heat treatment was 800°C/1h/WQ+600°C/2h/AC. The large dimples are associated with titanium carbide particles and the small dimples associated with alpha precipitates. The observation of sulphide precipitates on the fracture surfaces in samples with different solution treatment and ageing conditions is summarised in Table 2 . It can be seen from the table that sulphide precipitation occurs during ageing at 400 and 500°C, regardless of the solution treatment conditions investigated here. However, a significant difference was observed in samples aged at 600°C with different solution treatments. No sulphide precipitation was observed with a solution temperature of 800°C, a small number of precipitates (arrowed in Fig. 7c) were observed with a solution temperature of 900°C but obvious sulphide precipitation was observed in the samples solution treated at 1150°C as shown in Fig. 7d. Thus, high temperature solution treatment promotes sulphide precipitation during subsequent ageing. The details of the long features associated with sulphide precipitates on the fracture surfaces are shown in Fig. 8 . They are actually grooves with a thin line of the bottom of each of them. It is very likely that they were formed in a similar manner to that of forming normal dimples in ductile metallic materials but the soft matrix splits apart along the long thin precipitates. The precipitates forming those long grooves were with their longest direction nearly perpendicular to the tension direction and their broad surface nearly parallel to the tension direction during bending.

Sulphide/phosphide precipitation has not been an issue in commercial titanium alloys since the S and P concentration is very limited and the data reported here for the standard low C (<0.03wt%) Ti-153 is consistent with this. These elements, S, P and C are presumably well within the their solubility in titanium alloys at these ppm levels. However, with the development of low cost titanium alloys, master alloys used in steels industries are used in titanium industries. That will inevitably increase the concentration of unwanted interstitials. However, the S concentration is still under 10wtppm which is the lower limit of the analysis used in this study. As noted above sulphide/phosphide precipitation was not observed in a base Ti-153 alloy containing about 90wtppm C.

It can be seen by carefully analyzing the experimental results that there is a common factor, the solution treatment temperature, affecting sulphide precipitation during both continuous cooling and isothermal ageing. After continuous cooling in furnace to 700°C, sulphide precipitation was only observed in samples having subjected to a solution treatment at 1275 and 1150°C but not in those solutions treated at 900°C. It can be seen as well that with increasing solution treatment temperature, the density of sulphide precipitates is increased after furnace cooling to 700°C by comparing Figs. 4b and 6a and b. There should be a critical solution treatment temperature, below which no sulphide precipitation will occur under the same cooling condition used. Apparently, this temperature is between 1150 and 900°C for the specific continuous cooling condition. To estimate the critical temperature, sulphide precipitates density (total length per unit area) was measured and plotted against solution temperature in Fig. 9 where a linear relation is presumed. The intercept is found to be about 950°C.

Carbon has a low solubility in heavily alloyed beta alloys so that most of carbon will be in the form of carbide when cooling down from high temperature [9]. However, there is always some carbon retained in solution during cooling. With more carbon in solution at solution treatment temperature, more carbon may be retained when being cooled at the same cooling rate provided the cooling rate is not too low. In a multi-component solution, the solubility of one species is affected by other solutes and normally is reduced. This is likely to be true regardless of substitutional solutes or interstitials. For example, in the Ti–Mo system, the carbon solubility at 1000°C is reduced to 0.05wt% by 3wt% Mo from 0.125wt% without Mo [10,11]. It is reasonable to apply the same principle to sulphur and phosphorus, but in this case it is the carbon solute, which reduces the solubility of sulphur and phosphorus. This proposed mechanism is supported by the ageing results, especially those at 600°C in which case the obvious relationship between the occurrence of sulphide precipitates and the solution treatment temperature points to the carbon solution again. From Table 2, it can be seen clearly that increase in solution temperature pushes the occurrence of sulphide precipitation to high ageing temperature. This can be explained as follows: the driving force for sulphide precipitation during ageing is increased by the high carbon concentration in solution through reducing sulphur solubility and the required degree of undercooling is, therefore, reduced.

The following conclusions can be drawn from this study:
1.
The solubility of sulphur and phosphorus in Ti-153 is reduced in the presence of carbon at levels of 0.2wt%.
2.
The sulphur forms thin, plate-like Ti3S precipitates with an obvious crystallographic relation with the bcc matrix and the interfaces between the matrix and precipitates are preferred fracture paths.
3.
The density of these precipitates for defined cooling conditions increases with increasing the solution treatment temperature because the amount of C in solution increases at high temperatures.
4.
Since the addition of C accelerates precipitation of alpha leading to increased kinetics of precipitation hardening the level of C additions and the heat treatment schedule need to be optimised if C addition is to be used since the sulphide precipitates appear to initiate fracture.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like thank the EPSRC for funding this work through Grant Number GR/R64704/01 and Dr. S.P. Fox of Timet Henderson, USA and Dr. A. Wilson of Timet, UK, for the provision of the experimental materials. The constant encouragement, constructive criticism and stimulating discussions from/with Prof M.H. Loretto throughout this work is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: J. DelPrado, Ph.D. Thesis, The University of Birmingham, 2005.
V.N. Eremenko, V.E. Lismovnichii, 1967, quoted in [6].
D. Hu, A.J. Huang, X.P. Song, X. Wu, Precipitation in Carbon-Doped Ti–15V–3Cr–3Sn–3Al Alloys, will be Presented in Xi’an International Titanium Conference, October 2005.
J. Rausch, F.A. Crossley, 1954, quoted in [9].
